figures.caption: Fig. 4. Perturbation recovery from the induced flow. The policy predicts a spline from the observation, and the spline-to-field transformation constructs the corresponding flow field. When disturbances move the system away from the nominal motion, the perturbed states enter the flow field and are guided back toward the spline geometry.

Fig. 14. Spline Policy (Traj.) - PI05 on the PushT task under randomized initial positions. The language instruction is “Push the T-shape block into a green T marker”. Left to right: snapshots of the ALOHA robot executing the task with different initial configurations of the T-block.

Fig. 1. Overview of SP as a structured output interface. Top-left: An observation is processed by an existing policy backbone, while the output representation is replaced by spline parameters. Top-right: Directly predicting spline parameters via end-to-end training. Bottom: The same spline output supports complementary uses. As a trajectory representation, it provides compact, smooth temporal decoding; as a flow-field realization, it can be transformed into a local state-dependent correction mechanism via a spline-to-field construction.
Fig. 6. Null-space projection. The final command q̇action combines a highpriority collision-avoidance velocity q̇col with the SP velocity projected into the null space of the avoidance direction.

Fig. 12. Real-world compatibility case studies. Row 1: Vision-conditioned planning under changing spatial configurations. Row 2: Recovery under repeated pushing and pulling disturbances. Row 3: Whole-body collision avoidance via null-space projection in the presence of an undesired object. Row 4: Structured motion specification and disturbance trials with a liquid-filled glass.

Fig. 8. Comparison between Baseline model and Spline Policy (Flow) (ours) across three kinds of demonstrations: Snake, G-shape, and Sine. The purple observations indicate perturbed states. The gray ellipses highlight that the baseline model breaks spatial consistency under perturbations.
Fig. 9. Qualitative pushing illustration of Baseline model and Spline Policy (Flow) (ours) under perturbed initial states.
Fig. 11. Trajectory continuity comparison between Baseline model and Spline Policy (Traj.) (ours) under replanning.

Fig. 10. Benchmark between Baseline model and Spline Policy (Traj.) (ours) on tasks spanning state, vision, and point-cloud inputs. Test scores and network-level relative FLOPs are averaged over six tasks.
